questions.stem: According to cell theory, cells are:

options.text: The basic structural unit of all living organisms

questions.explanation: Cell theory states that cells are the basic structural unit of all living organisms.

questions.stem: Using cell theory, what can be deductively predicted about it?

options.text: It will consist of one or more cells
It will be made of pure protein

questions.explanation: Deductive reasoning from cell theory predicts a newly discovered organism will consist of one or more cells.

questions.scenario: A student measures the size of cells under a microscope.
questions.stem: Which tool is used to measure cell sizes with a light microscope?

options.text: A ruler placed on the screen

questions.explanation: Sizes are measured using an eyepiece graticule, which is calibrated against a stage micrometer.

questions.scenario: A drawing of a cell is 50 mm wide, and the cell's actual width is 0.05 mm.
questions.stem: What is the magnification of the drawing?
questions.explanation: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size = 50 ÷ 0.05 = ×1000.

questions.scenario: A cell appears 40 mm long in a photo taken at ×2000 magnification.
questions.stem: What is the actual length of the cell?
questions.explanation: Actual size = image size ÷ magnification = 40 mm ÷ 2000 = 0.02 mm = 20 µm.

questions.stem: A major advantage of electron microscopy over light microscopy is:

options.text: It shows living cells in natural colour

questions.explanation: Electron microscopy has far higher resolution, revealing fine detail that light microscopes cannot.

questions.stem: Immunofluorescence in light microscopy is used to:

options.text: Locate specific molecules using fluorescent markers
Increase the mass of the cell
Replace the need for any stain

questions.explanation: Fluorescent stains and immunofluorescence let specific molecules be located and imaged in light microscopy.

questions.stem: Which feature is common to the cells of ALL living organisms?
questions.explanation: All cells have DNA, cytoplasm mainly of water, and a plasma membrane made of lipids; not all have walls, chloroplasts or nuclei.

questions.stem: The cytoplasm of a typical cell is composed mainly of:
questions.explanation: Cytoplasm common to all cells is composed mainly of water, the medium for life's processes.

questions.stem: The plasma membrane of all cells is composed mainly of:
questions.explanation: All cells are enclosed by a plasma membrane composed of lipids.

questions.stem: Which type of ribosome is found in prokaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes have smaller 70S ribosomes, whereas eukaryotes have 80S ribosomes in their cytoplasm.

questions.stem: How is the DNA of a prokaryotic cell arranged?

options.text: As naked DNA in a loop, not in a nucleus

questions.explanation: Prokaryotes have naked DNA in a loop, without a nuclear membrane or histones.

questions.stem: Which of these is a component of a prokaryotic cell?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells have a cell wall, plasma membrane, cytoplasm, naked looped DNA and 70S ribosomes, but no membrane-bound organelles.

questions.stem: Which type of ribosome is found in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cytoplasm contains 80S ribosomes, larger than the 70S ribosomes of prokaryotes.

questions.stem: In a eukaryotic cell, the DNA in the nucleus is bound to which proteins?
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic chromosomes are made of DNA bound to histone proteins, contained in a double membrane with pores.

questions.stem: The nuclear envelope of a eukaryotic cell is described as:

options.text: A single membrane with no pores

questions.explanation: The nucleus is enclosed by a double membrane containing pores, allowing exchange with the cytoplasm.

questions.stem: Which of these is a membrane-bound organelle found in eukaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: Eukaryotes have membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria, ER, Golgi apparatus and vesicles.

questions.stem: The cytoskeleton of eukaryotic cells is made of:
questions.explanation: The eukaryotic cytoskeleton is composed of microtubules and microfilaments.

questions.scenario: A single-celled organism carries out all life processes within one cell.
questions.stem: Which of these is a function of life shown by unicellular organisms?
questions.explanation: Unicellular organisms carry out functions of life such as homeostasis, metabolism, nutrition, movement, excretion, growth, response and reproduction.

questions.stem: Which list correctly names functions of life shown by unicellular organisms?
questions.explanation: The functions of life include homeostasis, metabolism, nutrition, movement, excretion, growth, response to stimuli and reproduction.

questions.stem: Which structure is present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are found in plant cells (and some protists) but not in animal or fungal cells.

questions.stem: The cell wall of a plant cell is composed mainly of cellulose, whereas a fungal cell wall is made of:
questions.explanation: Animal, fungal and plant cells differ in cell wall composition: plants use cellulose while fungi use chitin; animal cells have no wall.

questions.stem: Centrioles, cilia and flagella are typically associated with which cell type?
questions.explanation: Presence of centrioles, cilia and flagella is one of the features distinguishing animal cells from fungi and plants.

questions.stem: A mature mammalian red blood cell is atypical because it:
questions.explanation: Red blood cells are atypical because they lack a nucleus, an example of variation in nuclei number.

questions.stem: Skeletal muscle fibres are described as atypical cells because they:
questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle fibres are multinucleate, illustrating atypical cell structure based on nuclei number.

questions.stem: Which is an example of atypical eukaryotic cell structure using nuclei number?

options.text: Aseptate fungal hyphae with many nuclei
A typical liver cell with one nucleus

questions.explanation: Aseptate fungal hyphae contain many nuclei in a continuous cytoplasm, an example of atypical structure.

questions.scenario: A micrograph shows a cell with a nucleus, mitochondria and no cell wall.
questions.stem: This cell is most likely a:
questions.explanation: A eukaryotic cell with a nucleus and mitochondria but no cell wall or chloroplasts is an animal cell.

questions.scenario: A micrograph shows a cell with a nucleus, chloroplasts, a large central vacuole and a cell wall.
questions.stem: This cell is a:
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts, a large sap vacuole and a cell wall together identify a plant cell.

questions.scenario: An electron micrograph shows a small cell with a nucleoid region and no membrane-bound organelles.
questions.stem: This cell is:
questions.explanation: A nucleoid region and absence of membrane-bound organelles identify a prokaryotic cell.

questions.scenario: A student annotates a diagram of a mitochondrion from an electron micrograph.
questions.stem: According to the syllabus, the annotation must include the organelle's:
questions.explanation: Students are required to include functions in their annotations of organelles and structures.

questions.stem: Which organelle is responsible for modifying and packaging proteins for secretion?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus modifies and packages proteins, often into secretory vesicles.

questions.stem: Lysosomes are best described as:
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicles containing digestive enzymes, part of the eukaryotic organelle system.

questions.scenario: The syllabus uses Gram-positive eubacteria as the model prokaryote.
questions.stem: Which of these is an example given?
questions.explanation: The required prokaryote structure is that of Gram-positive eubacteria such as Bacillus and Staphylococcus.

questions.stem: Cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM) is a development that allows:

options.text: Imaging of rapidly frozen samples in near-native state
Viewing living cells in colour with the naked eye

questions.explanation: Cryo-EM is one of the microscopy developments, imaging rapidly frozen samples at high resolution.

questions.scenario: A student prepares cells to view under a light microscope.
questions.stem: Making a 'temporary mount' involves:

options.text: Placing a specimen on a slide, often with stain and a coverslip
Permanently sealing the specimen in resin

questions.explanation: Microscopy skills include making temporary mounts of cells and tissues, often with staining and a coverslip.

questions.stem: Which molecule serves as the genetic material common to all cells?
questions.explanation: All cells have DNA as their genetic material, along with cytoplasm and a plasma membrane.

questions.stem: Compared with a small vacuole in an animal cell, a plant cell typically has:
questions.explanation: Differences in the size and function of vacuoles distinguish plant cells, which have a large central sap vacuole.

questions.scenario: A student views an electron micrograph and must identify structures.
questions.stem: Which of these can be identified in an electron micrograph but not resolved by light microscopy?

options.text: The nucleus of a large cell

questions.explanation: Fine structures such as ribosomes require the higher resolution of electron microscopy to be seen.

questions.stem: The rough endoplasmic reticulum is 'rough' because it is studded with:
questions.explanation: Rough ER is covered with ribosomes, giving it a rough appearance and a role in protein synthesis.

questions.scenario: A unicellular organism responds to a change in light.
questions.stem: This behaviour is which function of life?
questions.explanation: Reacting to an environmental change such as light is the function of life called response to stimuli.

questions.stem: According to the theory of endosymbiosis, mitochondria originated as:

options.text: Free-living prokaryotes taken up by a host cell

questions.explanation: Endosymbiosis proposes mitochondria evolved from free-living prokaryotes engulfed by an ancestral eukaryote.

questions.stem: Which feature of mitochondria and chloroplasts supports the endosymbiotic theory?

options.text: They contain 70S ribosomes and naked circular DNA
They have 80S ribosomes like the cytoplasm

questions.explanation: The presence of 70S ribosomes, naked circular DNA and the ability to replicate all point to a prokaryotic origin.

questions.stem: The endosymbiotic origin of chloroplasts is thought to have occurred:

options.text: After mitochondria, in some eukaryote lineages

questions.explanation: Mitochondria arose first by endosymbiosis; chloroplasts then had an endosymbiotic origin in some eukaryotes.

questions.scenario: A theory explains a wide range of observations and supports many predictions.
questions.stem: What does this indicate about the theory of endosymbiosis?

options.text: Its strength comes from the many observations it explains
It must be false because it is old

questions.explanation: A theory's strength comes from the observations it explains and predictions it supports, as with endosymbiosis.

questions.stem: Cell differentiation in multicellular organisms is based on:
questions.explanation: Differentiation results from different patterns of gene expression, often triggered by environmental changes.

questions.scenario: Two body cells contain the same genes but perform very different roles.
questions.stem: How is this possible?

options.text: They express different subsets of their genes

questions.explanation: Cells with identical genomes differentiate because they express different genes.

questions.stem: Which statement about the evolution of multicellularity is correct?

options.text: It has evolved repeatedly in different lineages
It evolved only once, in animals

questions.explanation: Multicellularity has evolved repeatedly, appearing in many fungi, algae, and all plants and animals.

questions.stem: Which is an advantage of multicellularity?

options.text: It allows larger body size and cell specialization
It removes the need for a plasma membrane

questions.explanation: Multicellularity gives the advantages of larger body size and specialization of cells.

questions.stem: The common ancestor of all eukaryotes is thought to have already had:

options.text: A nucleus and the ability to reproduce sexually

questions.explanation: Evidence suggests the common eukaryote ancestor had a nucleus and reproduced sexually before mitochondria arose by endosymbiosis.

questions.stem: Cell differentiation is often triggered by:

options.text: The complete loss of the nucleus

questions.explanation: Different patterns of gene expression that drive differentiation are often triggered by environmental changes.
